description:
Create Alembic migration adding shared.entity_info table parallel to contact_info
but FK'd to entities instead of contacts.

Schema:
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
  entity_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES shared.entities(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
  type        VARCHAR NOT NULL
  value       TEXT NOT NULL
  label       VARCHAR
  is_primary  BOOLEAN DEFAULT false
  secured     BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
  UNIQUE (entity_id, type)  -- one value per type per entity

Key differences from contact_info:
- FK to entities (not contacts)
- UNIQUE on (entity_id, type) not (type, value) — entity credentials are
  identity-scoped, not globally unique channel identifiers
- No parent_id column (entity secrets don't nest)

Files to modify:
- New: alembic/versions/core/core_0XX_entity_info.py

The module-contacts spec (Requirement: TelegramContactsProvider, Scenario: Telegram full sync) states:
  external_id = "telegram:<user_id>" (e.g., "telegram:123456789")

But the implementation in telegram_provider.py:83 uses:
  external_id=user_id  (just the bare numeric string, e.g., "123456789")

This affects contacts_source_links uniqueness. While it works correctly in single-provider mode (provider column disambiguates), the bare format is inconsistent with the spec's namespaced convention that prevents external_id collisions across future providers that might also use numeric IDs.

**Fix**: Change telegram_provider.py _user_to_canonical() to use f"telegram:{user_id}" for external_id. Note: existing contacts_source_links rows will need a data migration or the sync engine will create duplicate entries on first run after the fix.

**Files**: src/butlers/modules/contacts/telegram_provider.py:83
title: Telegram external_id format mismatch with spec

description:
The contacts module currently only supports Google Contacts as a sync source. Telegram contacts are a valuable identity source — the user's Telegram contact list contains phone numbers, usernames, and chat IDs that enable cross-provider identity resolution and richer disambiguation.

This epic adds Telegram as a second contacts provider, updating the multi-provider architecture and ensuring contacts from both sources are properly disambiguated via phone number and name matching. Telegram-specific fields (username/handle, user ID, chat ID) must be stored in shared.contact_info to support reverse-lookup and Switchboard identity resolution.

The Telethon dependency and user-client credential infrastructure (telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session in shared.contact_info) already exist for the Telegram user client connector — this work extends that foundation to contact syncing.
title: Add Telegram contacts sync provider

description:
Systematic migration of dedicated CRUD storage tables across butlers to use the bitemporal SPO facts system (from bu-axm). Once bu-axm lands valid_at + is_temporal support on the facts table, other domain tables that store event logs or simple property data can be rewritten as fact wrappers — gaining cross-butler visibility, semantic search, entity anchoring, and lifecycle management for free.

## Entity Anchoring Contract

All facts MUST be anchored to a resolved entity_id via memory_entity_resolve(). Subjects are NEVER generic strings like "user" — they are either:
- A resolved entity UUID (owner, a known contact, a named person)
- An anonymous placeholder entity created via memory_entity_create() for unresolved actors

The owner entity is bootstrapped at daemon startup and available via shared.contacts WHERE roles @> '["owner"]'. All self-referencing health/finance data uses the owner's entity_id.

## Scope — Tables to Migrate

### Phase 1: Health Butler (depends on bu-axm)
Already storing temporal event data with simple schemas:
- measurements → entity_id + measurement_{type} predicate + value in metadata + valid_at
- symptoms → entity_id + symptom predicate + severity/notes in metadata + valid_at  
- medication_doses → entity_id + took_dose predicate + medication ref in metadata + valid_at
- medications → entity_id + medication predicate + dosage/frequency/schedule in metadata (property fact, supersession)
- conditions → entity_id + condition predicate + status in metadata (property fact, supersession)
- research → entity_id + research predicate + content/tags/source_url in metadata (property fact)

### Phase 2: Relationship Butler
Contact-anchored data that maps naturally to entity-anchored facts:
- quick_facts → already SPO-shaped (contact → key → value); migrate to entity_id + predicate + content
- interactions → entity_id + interaction_{type} predicate + summary + valid_at
- life_events → entity_id + life_event predicate + summary + valid_at
- notes → entity_id + note predicate + content + valid_at (append-only temporal)
- gifts → entity_id + gift predicate + description/status in metadata (property fact)
- loans → entity_id + loan predicate + amount/direction in metadata (property fact)
- tasks → entity_id + task predicate + title/completed in metadata (property fact)
- reminders → entity_id + reminder predicate + message/cron in metadata (property fact)
- activity_feed → entity_id + activity predicate + description + valid_at (temporal)

### Phase 3: Finance Butler
Transaction and account data with entity anchoring:
- transactions → owner entity_id + transaction_{direction} predicate + merchant/amount/category in metadata + valid_at=posted_at (temporal)
- accounts → owner entity_id + account predicate + institution/type/last_four in metadata (property fact)
- subscriptions → owner entity_id + subscription predicate + service/amount/status in metadata (property fact)
- bills → owner entity_id + bill predicate + payee/amount/due_date in metadata (property fact)

### Phase 4: Home Butler
- ha_entity_snapshot → entity_id (HA device entity) + ha_state predicate + state/attributes in metadata (property fact with supersession on each snapshot)

### NOT migrating (structural/relational/operational)
- Education mind maps (graph structure with spaced repetition)
- Travel trips/legs/accommodations (parent-child hierarchy)
- Delivery pipeline (state machines with retry logic)
- Approval rules/pending actions (state machines)
- Sync infrastructure (cursor/etag tracking)
- Configuration registries (triage rules, routing instructions)
- Taxonomy seed tables (life_event_categories, relationship_types)

## Predicate Registry Seeding

Each phase seeds new predicates into predicate_registry with is_temporal flag:
- Temporal: measurement_weight, measurement_blood_pressure, symptom, took_dose, interaction_call, life_event, transaction_debit, transaction_credit, etc.
- Property: medication, condition, research, account, subscription, bill, gift, loan, task, reminder, ha_state, etc.

## Migration Strategy

Each table migration follows this pattern:
1. Spec update (openspec delta)
2. Seed predicates into registry (migration)
3. Rewrite tool functions as fact wrappers (query facts table instead of dedicated table)
4. Update MCP tool registrations
5. Data migration script (backfill existing rows into facts)
6. Deprecate old table (do not drop yet)
7. Update tests

## Aggregation Queries

Tools like nutrition_summary, spending_summary, trend_report currently use SQL aggregation on dedicated tables. After migration, these become metadata JSONB queries on facts:
  SELECT metadata->>'calories' FROM facts WHERE predicate LIKE 'meal_%' AND valid_at BETWEEN ...
Performance consideration: add a GIN index on facts.metadata for JSONB queries, and consider a partial index on predicate for high-volume temporal predicates.

## Out of Scope
- Dropping old tables (deprecate only — drop in a future cleanup epic)
- Changing the memory consolidation engine for temporal facts
- Migrating education, travel, delivery, or approval tables

2. Metadata schema conventions — standardize how structured data (JSONB nutrition, NUMERIC amounts, INTEGER severity) is stored in fact metadata. Define a metadata schema per predicate family (e.g., measurement predicates always have {value: ..., unit: ...}).

description:
Migrate 6 remaining health tables to bitemporal SPO facts. All facts anchored to owner entity_id (resolved from shared.contacts WHERE roles @> '["owner"]').

Temporal tables (event-per-row, is_temporal=true predicates):
- measurements → predicate measurement_{type} (measurement_weight, measurement_blood_pressure, measurement_heart_rate, measurement_blood_sugar, measurement_temperature). Content = human-readable summary. Metadata = {value: <JSONB>, unit: <str>, notes: <str>}. valid_at = measured_at.
- symptoms → predicate symptom. Content = symptom name. Metadata = {severity: <int 1-10>, condition_id: <uuid|null>, notes: <str>}. valid_at = occurred_at.
- medication_doses → predicate took_dose. Content = medication name. Metadata = {medication_id: <uuid>, skipped: <bool>, notes: <str>}. valid_at = taken_at.

Property tables (one-active-per-entity, supersession, is_temporal=false):
- medications → predicate medication. Content = "{name} {dosage} {frequency}". Metadata = {name, dosage, frequency, schedule: <list>, active: <bool>, notes}. Subject = medication name for searchability.
- conditions → predicate condition. Content = "{name}: {status}". Metadata = {name, status, diagnosed_at, notes}. Subject = condition name.
- research → predicate research. Content = research content text. Metadata = {title, tags: <list>, source_url, condition_id}. Subject = research title.

For each table:
1. Seed predicates into predicate_registry (new migration)
2. Rewrite tool functions in roster/health/tools/ as fact wrappers
3. Update MCP tool registrations in roster/health/modules/tools.py
4. Update report tools (health_summary, trend_report) to query facts
5. Update tests in roster/health/tests/test_tools.py
6. Write data migration script for existing rows

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 6 tables' tools read/write facts instead of dedicated tables
2. Predicate registry seeded with all health predicates
3. measurement_history, symptom_history, medication_history return same response shapes
4. health_summary and trend_report work against facts
5. All existing tests pass with updated implementations

description:
Migrate 9 relationship butler tables to bitemporal SPO facts. All facts anchored to the relevant contact's entity_id (resolved via shared.contacts → shared.entities FK, NOT generic 'user').

Temporal tables (is_temporal=true):
- interactions → predicate interaction_{type}. Content = summary. Metadata = {type, notes}. valid_at = occurred_at. entity_id = contact's entity.
- life_events → predicate life_event. Content = summary. Metadata = {life_event_type, description}. valid_at = happened_at. entity_id = contact's entity.
- notes → predicate contact_note. Content = note content. Metadata = {emotion}. valid_at = created_at. entity_id = contact's entity. (Append-only, no supersession.)
- activity_feed → predicate activity. Content = description. Metadata = {type}. valid_at = created_at. entity_id = contact's entity.

Property tables (is_temporal=false, supersession):
- quick_facts → predicate = the key itself (dynamic predicate). Content = value. entity_id = contact's entity. (Already SPO-shaped — most natural migration.)
- gifts → predicate gift. Content = description. Metadata = {occasion, status}. entity_id = contact's entity.
- loans → predicate loan. Content = description. Metadata = {amount, direction, settled, settled_at}. entity_id = contact's entity.
- tasks → predicate contact_task. Content = title. Metadata = {description, completed, completed_at}. entity_id = contact's entity.
- reminders → predicate reminder. Content = message. Metadata = {reminder_type, cron, due_at, dismissed}. entity_id = contact's entity.

Entity resolution: Each contact_id FK maps to a shared.contacts row which has an entity_id FK to shared.entities. The fact's entity_id is this resolved entity UUID.

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 9 tables' tools read/write facts instead of dedicated tables
2. Predicate registry seeded with relationship predicates
3. All tool response shapes preserved (backward compatible)
4. quick_facts migration is cleanest (key→predicate, value→content)
5. Tests updated and passing
title: Migrate relationship butler CRUD tables to SPO facts

description:
Migrate 4 finance butler tables to bitemporal SPO facts. All facts anchored to owner entity_id for self-owned financial data.

Temporal tables (is_temporal=true):
- transactions → predicate transaction_{direction} (transaction_debit, transaction_credit). Content = "{merchant} {amount} {currency}". Metadata = {account_id, source_message_id, merchant, description, amount (string-encoded NUMERIC), currency, category, payment_method, receipt_url, external_ref, extra metadata}. valid_at = posted_at. entity_id = owner entity.

Property tables (is_temporal=false, supersession):
- accounts → predicate account. Content = "{institution} {type} ****{last_four}". Metadata = {institution, type, name, last_four, currency, extra metadata}. entity_id = owner entity. Uniqueness key = entity_id + scope + predicate + content (since multiple accounts exist — may need per-account entity or content-based differentiation).
- subscriptions → predicate subscription. Content = "{service} {amount}/{frequency}". Metadata = {service, amount, currency, frequency, next_renewal, status, auto_renew, payment_method, account_id, source_message_id}. entity_id = owner entity.
- bills → predicate bill. Content = "{payee} {amount} due {due_date}". Metadata = {payee, amount, currency, due_date, frequency, status, payment_method, account_id, statement_period_start/end, paid_at, source_message_id}. entity_id = owner entity.

Key challenges:
- Transaction deduplication: current uq_transactions_dedupe partial index on (source_message_id, merchant, amount, posted_at). Need equivalent dedup logic in store_fact or a metadata-based unique constraint.
- NUMERIC precision: transaction amounts are NUMERIC(14,2). Storing as string in metadata JSONB loses type safety. Consider storing amount as string and parsing in aggregation queries.
- spending_summary aggregation: currently SQL SUM/GROUP BY on transactions table. After migration, becomes JSONB extraction + aggregation on facts.

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 4 tables' tools read/write facts instead of dedicated tables
2. Transaction deduplication preserved (no duplicate ingestion from same source message)
3. spending_summary returns correct aggregations from fact metadata
4. NUMERIC precision maintained through string encoding in metadata
5. Tests updated and passing
title: Migrate finance butler CRUD tables to SPO facts

description:
Migrate ha_entity_snapshot (1 table) to bitemporal SPO facts.

- ha_entity_snapshot → predicate ha_state. Content = state value (e.g. "on", "22.5"). Metadata = {attributes: <JSONB>, entity_id_ha: <HA entity string>}. entity_id = resolved HA device entity (or auto-created entity per HA device). valid_at = last_updated from HA. Property fact with supersession (each snapshot upserts = supersedes previous).

Key consideration: HA entities are not people — they're devices/sensors. May need entity_type expansion beyond person/organization/place/other, or use 'other' with metadata.entity_class = 'ha_device'.

Lower priority than health/relationship/finance — the snapshot table is small and read-only from the butler's perspective.

Acceptance criteria:
1. ha_entity_snapshot reads/writes facts instead of dedicated table
2. Entity resolution creates/reuses HA device entities in shared.entities
3. Supersession correctly replaces previous state on each snapshot cycle
4. Tests updated and passing

description:

1. GIN index on facts.metadata for general JSONB containment queries (@>, ?, ?|, ?&)
2. Partial B-tree indexes on commonly extracted metadata fields for high-volume predicates:
   - (predicate, valid_at) WHERE predicate LIKE 'meal_%' AND validity = 'active' — meal aggregation
   - (predicate, valid_at) WHERE predicate LIKE 'transaction_%' AND validity = 'active' — spending aggregation
   - (predicate, valid_at) WHERE predicate LIKE 'measurement_%' AND validity = 'active' — health trends
3. Benchmark aggregation query performance before and after indexes with realistic data volumes.

Write as a migration (mem_008 or later, after the valid_at migration lands).

Acceptance criteria:
1. GIN index on facts.metadata exists
2. At least 3 partial predicate indexes for high-volume temporal predicates
3. Aggregation queries (nutrition_summary, spending_summary) execute in <100ms for 10k facts
title: Add GIN indexes on facts.metadata for JSONB aggregation queries

description:
Write idempotent data migration scripts that backfill existing rows from deprecated CRUD tables into the facts table. One script per phase:

1. Health: measurements, symptoms, medication_doses, medications, conditions, research → facts
2. Relationship: quick_facts, interactions, life_events, notes, gifts, loans, tasks, reminders, activity_feed → facts
3. Finance: transactions, accounts, subscriptions, bills → facts
4. Home: ha_entity_snapshot → facts

Each script must:
- Resolve entity_id for each row (owner entity for health/finance, contact entity for relationship)
- Generate embeddings via EmbeddingEngine for each fact
- Set valid_at from the source timestamp column (eaten_at, measured_at, occurred_at, posted_at, etc.)
- Set appropriate permanence (stable for chronic/long-term, standard for current state, volatile for acute)
- Skip rows that already have a corresponding active fact (idempotent)
- Log progress and errors
- Be runnable as a management command (e.g., python -m butlers.scripts.backfill_facts --phase health)

Acceptance criteria:
1. All 4 phase scripts exist and are idempotent
2. Entity resolution is correct (no generic 'user' subjects)
3. Embeddings are generated for all backfilled facts
4. Scripts can be re-run safely without duplicating data
title: Write data backfill scripts for CRUD-to-facts migration

The backfill engine (backfill.py) does not correctly write Telegram-specific contact_info types to shared.contact_info during sync. Three sub-issues:

1. **telegram_user_id never created**: When a Telegram contact is synced, no contact_info entry with type='telegram_user_id' is created. The user_id is stored in external_id and contacts_source_links but not as a contact_info entry — so reverse-lookup by telegram_user_id is impossible.

2. **telegram_username stored incorrectly**: Telegram usernames are stored as type='other' with label='telegram' (backfill.py:444-446) instead of type='telegram_username'. Additionally, the value includes the '@' prefix (telegram_provider.py:71) but the contacts-identity spec says it should be stored without '@'.

3. **telegram_chat_id entries never populated**: TelegramContactsProvider.enrich_chat_ids() exists (telegram_provider.py:239) to resolve private chat IDs from dialogs, but it is never called from the sync pipeline (on_startup, sync engine, or backfill engine). No code path creates telegram_chat_id contact_info entries for synced contacts.

**Impact**: Cross-provider identity resolution via telegram_user_id is broken. Switchboard reverse-lookup by telegram_chat_id for synced contacts doesn't work. Username search by type='telegram_username' returns no results.

**Fix approach**: 
- Add post-sync hook in ContactsModule.on_startup or sync engine callback to:
  a) Create telegram_user_id contact_info entries from CanonicalContact.external_id for telegram provider
  b) Store usernames as type='telegram_username' (not 'other') without '@' prefix
  c) Call enrich_chat_ids() after sync to populate telegram_chat_id entries
- Alternatively, make the backfill engine provider-aware for contact_info type mapping.

**Files**: src/butlers/modules/contacts/backfill.py, src/butlers/modules/contacts/telegram_provider.py, src/butlers/modules/contacts/__init__.py

description:
Replace the health butler's dedicated `meals` table with temporal facts in the memory module's `facts` table. This unifies meal data into the SPO knowledge graph, making it visible cross-butler and queryable via semantic search.

## Motivation

The health butler currently stores meals in a purpose-built `meals` table (id, type, description, nutrition JSONB, eaten_at, notes). When asked "what's for lunch on 2026-03-18?", the butler queries `meal_history` which hits this table. But the memory module — which the health butler also loads — has no knowledge of meals. This creates two parallel data stores with no cross-visibility.

The `facts` table already supports subject/predicate/content with entity anchoring, embeddings, semantic search, and lifecycle management. Meals are a natural fit as temporal facts: `{entity} had_lunch {description} at {valid_at}`, with nutrition data in `metadata` JSONB.

## Key Design Decisions

### Bitemporality
The `facts` table gains a `valid_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()` column alongside the existing `created_at`. This captures two distinct time dimensions:
- `created_at` — when the fact was recorded (assertion time)
- `valid_at` — when the fact is true/occurred (valid time)

For property facts (e.g., "user's weight is 75kg"), both default to now(). For event facts (e.g., "user had lunch"), `valid_at` is the event time (which may be in the future for pre-orders).

### Temporal vs Property Fact Supersession
The existing supersession logic marks old facts as `superseded` when a new fact shares the same uniqueness key `(entity_id, scope, predicate)`. Event/temporal facts must NOT be superseded — multiple lunches on different days are independent facts. The `predicate_registry` gains an `is_temporal BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false` flag. When `is_temporal=true`, `store_fact` skips the supersession check.

### Unique Index Changes
The current unique index on `(entity_id, scope, predicate)` WHERE active prevents two active temporal facts with the same predicate. Adding `valid_at` to the index — `(entity_id, scope, predicate, valid_at)` — allows temporal facts to coexist at different times while the app-level supersession still handles property facts correctly (it marks old facts as superseded before inserting, so the index never sees two active property facts with the same key).

### Health Diet Tools as Wrappers
`meal_log`, `meal_history`, and `nutrition_summary` become thin wrappers over the facts table:
- `meal_log` → `store_fact` with predicate `meal_{type}`, nutrition in `metadata`, explicit `valid_at`
- `meal_history` → query facts by predicate pattern + `valid_at` range
- `nutrition_summary` → query facts, extract nutrition from `metadata` JSONB, aggregate

### Embedding Engine Access
The health module's diet tools need the memory module's `EmbeddingEngine` to call `store_fact`. Since both modules load in the same butler process, the diet tools can import from the memory module's helpers.

## Scope

### In scope
- `valid_at` column on `facts` table (migration mem_007)
- `is_temporal` flag on `predicate_registry`
- Meal predicates seeded into registry (`meal_breakfast`, `meal_lunch`, `meal_dinner`, `meal_snack`)
- `store_fact` API updated to accept `valid_at` and respect `is_temporal`
- `memory_store_fact` MCP tool updated with `valid_at` parameter
- Health diet tools (`diet.py`) rewritten as fact-query wrappers
- Health MCP tool registrations updated
- `trend_report` and `health_summary` updated to query facts instead of meals
- Tests updated

### Out of scope
- Migrating existing meal data from `meals` table to facts (can be a follow-up)
- Dropping the `meals` table (deprecate first, drop later)
- Applying bitemporality to other health tools (measurements, symptoms) — future work
- Changes to memory consolidation or decay logic for temporal facts
title: Bitemporal facts: replace health meals table with temporal SPO

description:
The module-contacts spec (Scenario: Telegram incremental sync) describes hash-based change detection:
- Provider computes a hash of the current contact list
- If hash matches the cursor, returns an empty batch (no changes detected)
- If hash differs, returns the full contact list

Current implementation (telegram_provider.py) uses a timestamp-based cursor and always returns the full contact list on every incremental sync. The sync engine's contact_versions hash comparison handles dedup, so the result is functionally correct.

**Impact**: Minor efficiency loss — every incremental sync serializes and returns all contacts even when nothing changed. In practice, this is negligible for typical contact list sizes (< 5000 contacts).

**Fix**: In incremental_sync(), compute a stable hash of the sorted contact list and compare with the incoming cursor. Return empty ContactBatch when hashes match.

**Files**: src/butlers/modules/contacts/telegram_provider.py
title: Telegram incremental sync should use hash-based cursor for optimization

description:
attached_molecule: bu-wisp-tpj7u
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-03-06T15:18:54Z
dispatched_by: mayor

All UUID generation across the codebase currently uses UUIDv4 (random). UUIDv7 (time-ordered, RFC 9562) is preferred because:
- **Sortable by creation time** — enables index-friendly insertion order on UUID PKs, reducing B-tree page splits
- **Implicit temporal ordering** — timestamp is embedded in the ID, useful for debugging and log correlation
- **No information loss** — still globally unique, still 128 bits

## Scope

### Python runtime (20 call sites)
Every `uuid.uuid4()` call should be replaced with a shared `uuid7()` helper. A private `_generate_uuid7_string()` already exists in `src/butlers/modules/pipeline.py:49` — promote it to a shared utility (e.g. `src/butlers/utils/ids.py`) and replace all call sites:

**src/butlers/modules/calendar.py** — 3 sites (lines ~3314, ~3349, ~6205)
**src/butlers/modules/memory/storage.py** — 3 sites (episode_id, fact_id, rule_id)
**src/butlers/modules/approvals/operations.py** — 2 sites (rule_id)
**src/butlers/modules/approvals/module.py** — 3 sites (rule_id)
**src/butlers/modules/approvals/gate.py** — 1 site (action_id)
**src/butlers/daemon.py** — 3 sites (request_id fallback, reminder task ID, action_id)
**src/butlers/core/route_inbox.py** — 1 site (row_id)
**src/butlers/connectors/heartbeat.py** — 1 site (instance_id)
**src/butlers/storage/blobs.py** — 1 site (unique_id)
**src/butlers/testing/migration.py** — 1 site (test DB name — uses hex slice, uuid7 fine here)
**roster/switchboard/api/router.py** — 1 site (backfill job_id)
**roster/switchboard/tools/dead_letter/replay.py** — 1 site (replay request_id)

### PostgreSQL schema defaults (~120 columns across 30+ migration files)
All `DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()` in migration files produce UUIDv4. Replace with a custom SQL function `shared.uuid_generate_v7()` created in a new shared migration, then update all subsequent defaults. PostgreSQL 17 does not have a native v7 generator; `pg_uuidv7` extension is not installed in the Docker image. Options:
1. **PL/pgSQL function** — pure SQL, no extension needed, runs in `shared` schema
2. **Install pg_uuidv7** — cleaner but requires Docker image change
3. **Application-side only** — generate UUIDv7 in Python, never rely on DB defaults (migration-heavy)

Recommended: option 1 (PL/pgSQL function) for new tables + a sweep migration that updates existing defaults.

### TypeScript/Frontend (3 call sites)
`crypto.randomUUID()` produces UUIDv4. Replace with a small `uuidv7()` utility. These are client-side IDs for request correlation; v7 adds implicit timestamp ordering for debugging.

**frontend/src/pages/CalendarWorkspacePage.tsx** — 2 sites
**frontend/src/components/butler-detail/ButlerTriggerTab.tsx** — 2 sites

### Existing UUIDv7 usage
`src/butlers/modules/pipeline.py` already has a correct `_generate_uuid7_string()` implementation with a Python 3.14+ `uuid.uuid7()` fast path. This should be promoted to a shared utility, not duplicated.

## Out of scope
- **Existing data** — no backfill of existing UUID PKs (would break FKs, not worth it)
- **UUID type annotations** — `uuid.UUID` type hints are version-agnostic, no changes needed
- **Test fixtures** — test-only UUID literals (e.g. `"test-calendar-id"`) are fine as-is
title: Migrate all UUID generation from v4 to UUIDv7

description:
attached_molecule: bu-wisp-hq94
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-03-06T04:17:28Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Add a TelegramContactsProvider that implements the ContactsProvider interface to sync contacts from the user's Telegram account via Telethon.

Key implementation details:

**Provider (sync.py or new telegram_provider.py)**:
- Implement ContactsProvider with name='telegram'
- Use Telethon client.get_contacts() to fetch the user's full Telegram contact list
- Credential resolution via resolve_owner_contact_info() for telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session (already stored in shared.contact_info)
- Full sync: fetch all contacts, return as ContactBatch with CanonicalContact instances
- Incremental sync: Telegram has no sync token API — fetch full list and compare against locally stored hashes (contact_versions in sync state) to detect additions/changes/deletions
- Map Telegram contact fields to CanonicalContact: first_name, last_name, phone → ContactPhone, username → ContactUsername

**Telegram-specific contact_info entries**:
- telegram_username: the @handle (e.g., '@alice') — stored as ContactUsername in CanonicalContact, written to shared.contact_info with type='telegram_username'
- telegram_user_id: numeric Telegram user ID (stable identifier) — stored in shared.contact_info with type='telegram_user_id'
- telegram_chat_id: private chat ID for this contact — requires calling client.get_input_entity() or resolving from dialogs — stored in shared.contact_info with type='telegram_chat_id'

**Disambiguation / Identity Resolution**:
- The existing ContactBackfillResolver resolution order (source_link → email → phone → name) handles cross-provider merging naturally:
  - Phone match: a Telegram contact with the same phone as a Google contact merges into the existing CRM record
  - Name match: conservative ILIKE match, ambiguous results flagged for dashboard disambiguation
- Telegram-specific contact_info rows are added to the matched/merged contact alongside Google-sourced entries
- Provenance tracked under metadata JSONB namespace 'sources.contacts.telegram.{field}'

**Module registration**:
- Add 'telegram' to ContactsModule._SUPPORTED_PROVIDERS
- Support provider config: allow provider='telegram' or multi-provider configuration in butler.toml
- Credential validation: verify Telethon session is valid before starting sync loop

**Sync runtime**:
- Telegram contact sync runs on the same polling interval as Google (configurable, default 15 min)
- Supports contacts_sync_now MCP tool trigger
- Sync state persisted with provider='telegram' in contacts_sync_state
design:
The TelegramContactsProvider should live alongside GoogleContactsProvider, either in sync.py or in a new telegram_provider.py if the file size warrants it. The Telethon client session should be created once during validate_credentials() and reused across sync cycles. The client must be properly disconnected on shutdown.

For chat_id resolution: Telethon's client.get_contacts() returns User objects with id and username but not the private chat_id. To get chat_ids, iterate client.iter_dialogs() and match by user_id. This can be done lazily or as a post-sync enrichment step to avoid blocking the main sync loop.

Multi-provider support: the simplest approach is allowing ContactsModule to instantiate multiple providers and run them in the same sync runtime. Each provider has independent sync state keyed by (provider, account_id). The ContactsSyncEngine already supports this via the provider parameter.
notes: Design decisions: (1) Create telegram_provider.py as separate file - sync.py is already large. (2) Telegram has no sync token, so full_sync returns all contacts with a timestamp-based cursor; incremental_sync fetches all contacts and engine's contact_versions hash handles dedup. (3) Credential resolution via resolve_owner_contact_info for telegram_api_id, telegram_api_hash, telegram_user_session. (4) list_groups returns empty GroupBatch (Telegram contacts don't have groups). (5) Chat ID resolution via iter_dialogs post-sync enrichment. (6) ContactsModule.on_startup gains elif branch for telegram provider.
title: Implement TelegramContactsProvider for contacts sync

3. Produce a checklist mapping every spec requirement to its implementing bead.
4. For any requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
   c. Do NOT create another reconciliation bead.
5. Keep this reconciliation bead open (or blocked) until all newly created gap beads are closed.
6. Re-run the requirement-to-bead checklist and close this bead only when all requirements show full coverage.

# Spec-to-Code Reconciliation: Telegram Contacts Sync

## Methodology
Compared specs (module-contacts/spec.md, contacts-identity/spec.md, connector-telegram-user-client/spec.md) against implementation (telegram_provider.py, contacts/__init__.py, backfill.py).

## Requirement Coverage Matrix

### module-contacts/spec.md

| Requirement | Bead | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ContactsModule Configuration (multi-provider `providers` list) | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Code uses single `provider` string, not `providers` list. No multi-provider config parsing. |
| Legacy single-provider config compat | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Current code IS the single-provider form |
| Unsupported provider at startup | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | on_startup() line 401-405 |
| Sync disabled behavior | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | on_startup() line 408-409 |
| Google OAuth Credential Resolution | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | lines 470-509 |
| Provider-Agnostic Sync Contract | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsProvider ABC in sync.py |
| Canonical Contact Model | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | CanonicalContact in sync.py |
| TelegramContactsProvider - credential resolution | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | _create_telegram_provider lines 511-560 |
| TelegramContactsProvider - full sync | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | telegram_provider.py lines 140-169 |
| TelegramContactsProvider - external_id format | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Spec: `"telegram:<user_id>"`, Code: bare `user_id` string |
| TelegramContactsProvider - incremental sync hash-based cursor | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Spec: hash-based with empty batch optimization. Code: timestamp cursor, always returns full list |
| TelegramContactsProvider - deletion detection | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | No tombstones, engine-level detection |
| TelegramContactsProvider - contact without phone | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Empty phones list |
| TelegramContactsProvider - validate_credentials | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Uses client.get_me() |
| TelegramContactsProvider - list_groups | bu-s21.2 | IMPLEMENTED | Returns empty GroupBatch |
| Multi-Provider Sync Runtime | — | **GAP** | Not implemented. Single _provider/_runtime. No independent loops, failure isolation, or per-provider MCP responses |
| Sync State Persistence | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsSyncStateStore |
| Sync Runtime with Polling Loop | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactsSyncRuntime |
| CRM Backfill Pipeline | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactBackfillEngine |
| Identity Resolution Pipeline | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | ContactBackfillResolver |
| MCP Tool Surface (4 tools) | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | Single-provider only |
| Shared Schema Tables | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | shared.contact_info |
| Cross-Provider Contact Backfill (Telegram+Google merge) | — | **PARTIAL** | Resolution pipeline supports it, but Telegram contact_info types not written correctly |
| Apple/CardDAV Provider | — | TARGET-STATE | Not current |

### contacts-identity/spec.md (Telegram-specific types)

| Requirement | Bead | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| telegram_user_id stored as contact_info | — | **GAP** | Backfill engine never creates telegram_user_id entries from external_id |
| telegram_username stored without @ prefix | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | Code stores `@username` (with @); spec says without @. Also stored as type='other' not 'telegram_username' |
| telegram_chat_id stored as contact_info | bu-s21.2 | **GAP** | enrich_chat_ids() exists but is never called from sync pipeline |
| Cross-provider disambiguation | pre-existing | IMPLEMENTED | Resolution order works for multi-provider via phone match |

## Identified Gaps (require new beads)

### Gap 1: Multi-provider configuration and runtime
Spec defines `providers` as a list. Code uses single `provider` string. No independent sync loops, failure isolation, or per-provider MCP tool responses.

### Gap 2: Telegram-specific contact_info types not populated by backfill
- telegram_user_id entries never created (backfill doesn't know about it)
- telegram_username stored as type='other' with label='telegram' instead of type='telegram_username'
- telegram_chat_id entries never populated (enrich_chat_ids() never called)
- Username value includes @ prefix (spec says without @)

### Gap 3: external_id format mismatch
Spec: `"telegram:<user_id>"`. Code: bare numeric string. Affects source_link matching uniqueness.

### Gap 4: Hash-based incremental sync cursor optimization
Spec says compute hash, return empty batch if unchanged. Code uses timestamp cursor and always returns full list. Functionally correct via engine dedup but misses optimization.
notes:
Reconciliation complete. 4 gap beads created under epic bu-s21:

1. bu-fxu (P1 bug): Telegram contact_info types not populated — telegram_user_id, telegram_username, telegram_chat_id entries missing or incorrect
2. bu-a6o (P2 bug): external_id format mismatch — bare user_id vs spec's "telegram:<user_id>" prefix
3. bu-j4t (P2 feature): Multi-provider config/runtime — single provider only, spec requires providers list with independent sync loops
4. bu-p4g (P3 task): Hash-based incremental sync cursor optimization — currently uses timestamp, always returns full list

bu-s21.3 is now blocked by all 4 gap beads. Will be closable when all gaps are resolved.

Key findings:
- Core TelegramContactsProvider (full_sync, validate_credentials, list_groups) is solid
- Credential resolution from owner contact_info works correctly
- Identity resolution pipeline is provider-agnostic and supports cross-provider merging
- The gaps are in the integration layer: how Telegram-specific data flows through the backfill engine into contact_info

description: Extend the current EAV-style entity/fact system into a labeled property graph by adding inter-entity edges. Today, facts describe properties of a single entity (subject-predicate-content anchored via entity_id). The system cannot express typed, traversable relationships between two entities. Goal: Add optional object_entity_id FK to facts so a fact can represent either a property (entity_id only) or an edge (entity_id + object_entity_id). Enable multi-hop graph traversal via recursive CTEs. Update specs, storage, tools, and LLM prompts. Non-goals: No SPARQL/OWL/formal ontology. No external graph database. No inference engine. Stay on PostgreSQL with asyncpg.
title: Transition entity/fact system to labeled property graph
